title: Myself
content:
There is a garden, grey
    With mists of autumntide;
 Under the giant boughs,
    Stretched green on every side,

 Along the lonely paths,
    A little child like me,
 With face, with hands, like mine,
    Plays ever silently;

 On, on, quite silently,
    When I am there alone,
 Turns not his head; lifts not his eyes;
    Heeds not as he plays on.

 After the birds are flown
    From singing in the trees,
 When all is grey, all silent,
    Voices, and winds, and bees;

 And I am there alone:
    Forlornly, silently,
 Plays in the evening garden
    Myself with me.
meaning:
About: Walter John de la Mare was an English poet, short story writer and novelist, best remembered for his works for children and "The Listeners".

He was born in Kent, and was educated at St Paul's Cathedral School.

His first book, Songs of Childhood, was published under the name Walter Ramal.

His 1921 novel, Memoirs of a Midget, won the James Tait Black Memorial Prize for fiction.
